item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heard the title of this SCP in a Sisters of Mercy song. DOMINION!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the description of this SCP, it states that subjects must be alone for SCP-3062 to target them. However, at the conclusion of the document, the entity targets multiple foundation personnel at once. Isn't this event contradictory to the nature of the SCP laid out in the description? Other than that, really enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The term "bailing out" might be more appropriate for a WWII context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As if the Sahara desert wasn’t scary enough…! Very nice ideas and good, chilling horror. There were a couple of small typos, but I fixed these for you.

My main criticism is that the log reads as though it were written by a modern American and not a 1940s RAF pilot. The language used is not appropriate at all. Whether you want to research the language of that period and class and employ some vernacular for better colour is of course up to you. At the very least, however, you should change the word “gasoline” to “petrol”.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't get a Satan vibe. I would expect Satan to offer a deal or otherwise attempt to corrupt it's victims. The reasonless malice combined with an Egyptian connection and an association with the deep desert made me think of Set.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: RAF planes (and nearly all WWII airplanes in general) did not have ejector seats and thus pilots could not "eject" from their planes. Pilots at the time had to climb out or flip the plane upside down and drop themselves out to escape, if they weren't incapacitated or the plane wasn't spiraling. Ejector seats weren't widely used until after the war, when jets took over the skies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What else would he be doing? I imagine the majority of his time would be spent just walking, and even then he only manages a paragraph each day.

In any case, this is a fun article — it's rare these days to have an SCP motivated by sheer malice that doesn't just parlay that into causing destruction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this is some reality-bending entity, pretending to be Satan? Or Satan itself? I immediately got what bible passage this was going to reference from the second paragraph, but I'm wary to upvote something that sounds mainly unchanged from its inspiration.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the concept and execution here, especially in the pilot's diary. 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my headcanon, this is what the Little Prince really was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed the concept and narrative, but the minor tone breaks and some other questionable prose bring it to a no-vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually give the "guy writing a diary entry to say he's being eaten by wolves" thing a pass any time they're in a profession that trains you to do that sort of compulsive record keeping. Military officers, pilots,inventors1, scientists2, explorers… there's a lot of professions where it's sort of "log books and paperwork or it didn't happen".

Footnotes
1. Your lawsuit has a lot more weight if you can present a stack of coffee-stained spiral notebooks with dated records of your work on an idea and the other guy can't.
2. ditto for scientific breakthroughs

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of figured he would write them at night. He has to sleep at some point, and documenting this kind of stuff in a diary seems like a thing a pilot in the 40s would do.

Anyway this was pretty good. There were a lot of places where the language was a little informal. The only thing I find weird is that you say "it has caused no direct fatalities through its actions", which might technically be true but the only 2 accounts we have of people encountering this thing, they both mysteriously disappear.
Still, I like the concept and the diary was decent enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would a guy stranded have so much time to write in a diary?

I agree. Though as I currently understand, an audio log or any other platform would've produced the same question anyway, so I guess a diary may be just as good as any other.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hadn't thought of that, but that's very likely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like the SCP's torment over James, but I thought to myself this: Why would a guy stranded have so much time to write in a diary?

But maybe it's part of the SCP's plan. Perhaps it wanted someone to scribe his deeds. After all, the ancient Egyptians did believe in maintaining a person's name (or deeds, in this case).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fourth skip ahoy!

Thanks to Tufto, MrWrong, AgentEGG, Vacuum, Fingo7, MaliceAF and everyone else who gave this a look through while in chat.

Image sources:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lone_man_in_a_hat_walking_in_the_Colorado_Desert_near_Palm_Springs,_1903-1904_(CHS-2281).jpg

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:AinManawirTemple.jpg

Hi cowman
